summary: DHH criticizes the practice of experienced programmers padding their questions with disclaimers when asking for help from less senior colleagues. He observes that accomplished programmers feel compelled to justify why they don't already know something, often writing disclaimers longer than the question itself. He argues these disclaimers are insulting to the person being asked and reflect grade-A insecurity. His conclusion is simple: if you need help, just ask for it.
key_points: Accomplished programmers often refuse to ask for help due to pride, or surround questions with elaborate disclaimers
Question disclaimers are a form of insecurity signaling that insults the person whose help is being sought
The disclaimers frequently exceed the length of the actual question, revealing misplaced priorities
Professional expertise does not require omniscience — it's okay not to know everything or to forget things
money_quotes: It's okay not to know everything, it's okay to forget -- except, apparently, if you're a highly accomplished programmer.
In that case your pride will demand a full-stop on all professional help from the underlings on the tech ladder in most cases.
And often you'll even surrounded the poor question with disclaimers both before and after. Just to make sure, of course.
Whatever the form, disclaimers are always insulting on the person who's help you're seeking.
So, could we please disband this practice of grade-A insecurity flashing?
If you need help just ask for it.
key_insight: When experienced programmers pad their questions with disclaimers about why they don't already know the answer, they reveal their own insecurity while insulting the person they're asking for help.
